tweets.name: Andrew D. Huberman, Ph.D.

reply_data.text: @timlouw1 @hubermanlab The caffeine-creatine antagonism is 1 study from 1996 in cyclists under a specific protocol. The actual meta-analysis data shows no meaningful interference on strength or power output. Split them if you get GI issues, but the concern itself is mostly legacy bro-science.

@Hammyscookie70 @hubermanlab The cramping from creatine is real for a subset of people and it is osmotic. Creatine pulls water into muscle cells rapidly, which can increase intramuscular pressure during intense output. Try splitting to 2.5g twice daily instead of one 5g dose. Smaller osmotic hit, same

@woodr3w @hubermanlab Creatine raises serum creatinine by ~30 micromol/L, which skews eGFR calculations downward. The fix is cystatin-C for kidney function, not stopping creatine. Get baseline cystatin-C before starting, recheck at 6 months. Tells you actual GFR without the noise.

@hubermanlab Honest question to everyone in the replies.  

How many of you are already taking creatine specifically for the cognitive side of things rather than the gym? Because the research on creatine and sleep deprivation recovery is genuinely underrated.

@hubermanlab Our body has such perfect chemical processes that it is capable of so much. The only problem our body has is when we follow the lead of our brain, which does not want to be reprogrammed myself to the desired rhythm, in which you will listen and hear the cues of your body.
@Swole_CPA @hubermanlab That's interesting, thank you for that, havent heard that before.

@SantangeloGreg @hubermanlab You’ve confused a routine with a system. A routine is just a habit; a system forces growth. If the weight isn't moving, your architecture is static. You’re maintaining a ruin instead of building a skyscraper.

@hubermanlab and how about those that dont? 
Its all subjective to the individual

@hubermanlab There’s genuine insight here - early adopters of creatine were vindicated, and the loading phase being unnecessary is now well-established. 

But the framing conflates two different things: the safety/efficacy of creatine (which is now among the most robustly evidenced
